serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Gong Bao Chicken is approximately 300 g, comprising diced chicken, peanuts, dried chillies, and sauce, usually shared as one dish among two to three diners alongside steamed rice.

nutrient_source.title: Kung Pao Chicken (Gong Bao Chicken) — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / Foundation Foods composite estimate
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA entry exists for Gong Bao Chicken as a complete dish. Values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy entries for cooked diced chicken breast (NDB 05064), roasted peanuts (NDB 16087), and standard stir-fry sauce components (soy sauce, sesame oil, sugar, cornstarch), weighted by a representative recipe ratio: ~55% chicken, ~10% peanuts, ~15% sauce/seasonings, ~20% vegetables and aromatics. Energy and macros cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central entry FDC ID 2103687 (Kung Pao Chicken, restaurant-type) where available. Values represent a plausible per-100 g composite for a restaurant-style preparation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; supplementary data for mixed Chinese stir-fry dishes with protein and fat
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Gong Bao Chicken. GI estimated at 35 based on analogous high-protein, moderate-fat Chinese stir-fry dishes with low net carbohydrate content (~6.5 g/100 g), consistent with mixed dishes containing chicken, peanuts, and a small amount of starch-thickened sauce. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (35 × 19.5) / 100 ≈ 6.8, where carbohydrate per 300 g serving = 6.5 × 3 = 19.5 g. GI of 35 is consistent with University of Sydney GI Database values for comparable protein-dominant stir-fry dishes (GI range 30–45). gi_category set to 'low' per threshold rule (GI ≤ 55).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce and seasoning (~480 mg/100 g; ~1440 mg per restaurant serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Gong Bao Chicken to small portions and request reduced-sodium soy sauce, as a single restaurant serving can contribute approximately 60% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~35) and moderate net carbohydrate content (~6.5 g/100 g) due to high protein and fat from chicken and peanuts
type2_diabetes.guidance: Gong Bao Chicken has a low glycemic index and moderate carbohydrate load, making it a relatively suitable protein-rich dish for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without large quantities of steamed rice and with attention to total portion size.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate total fat with a favourable unsaturated-to-saturated ratio from peanuts and vegetable oil, offset by high sodium from soy sauce
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While the fat profile of Gong Bao Chicken is relatively favourable due to peanut and vegetable oil content, the high sodium level (~1440 mg per serving) poses a meaningful cardiovascular risk and individuals with cardiovascular disease should request low-sodium preparation and limit portion size.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Gong Bao Chicken (宫保鸡丁, Gōng Bǎo Jī Dīng), also widely known as Kung Pao Chicken, is a classic Sichuan Chinese stir-fry dish consisting of diced chicken (typically breast or thigh), roasted peanuts, dried red chillies, Sichuan peppercorns, garlic, ginger, and spring onions, all tossed in a glossy, slightly sweet and tangy sauce made from soy sauce, rice vinegar, sugar, and cornstarch. The dish is cooked rapidly over high heat in a wok, producing tender chicken pieces with a characteristic numbing-spicy (麻辣, má là) flavour profile from the Sichuan peppercorns and chillies. The peanuts add crunch and a nutty richness that balances the heat. Gong Bao Chicken is one of the most internationally recognised Chinese dishes and is a staple of Chinese restaurant menus in Singapore and globally. Nutritionally, it is a moderate-energy, high-protein dish (~175 kcal and ~14.5 g protein per 100 g) with a low glycemic index (~35), making it relatively suitable for blood glucose management when consumed in appropriate portions. The primary nutritional concern is its high sodium content (~480 mg/100 g) from soy sauce, which is relevant for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish contains peanuts and soy, which are common allergens. It is typically served as a shared dish alongside steamed rice in Chinese restaurant settings.

meal_context: Shared dish in a multi-course Chinese meal
Lunch or dinner with steamed rice
Zi char (Singapore Chinese cooked food) menu item

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Gong Bao Chicken as a complete dish was found in a primary regional source (HPB Singapore Foodo or FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data for cooked chicken, roasted peanuts, and stir-fry sauce ingredients, weighted by a representative recipe ratio. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database comparable dishes. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level USDA data is high quality and the recipe composition of Gong Bao Chicken is well-standardised.
notes: 1. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source data for this dish type and is not expected to be a significant contributor in a stir-fried chicken preparation. 2. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero) as the dish uses vegetable oil and no hydrogenated fats; USDA component data confirms negligible trans fat in this preparation. 3. Nutrient values represent a per-100 g estimate for a restaurant-style Gong Bao Chicken preparation; actual values will vary by restaurant, recipe, and oil quantity used. 4. The cuisine_tags in the input JSON ('Seafood Dish', 'Crab', 'Lobster') appear to be erroneous tags from the source restaurant (Riverfront Seafood - Boat Quay) and do not describe Gong Bao Chicken; these tags have been disregarded. 5. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Gong Bao Chicken was located in the University of Sydney GI Database as of the access date. 6. Glycemic load per serving = (35 × 19.5) / 100 = 6.8, based on a 300 g serving containing 19.5 g carbohydrate.
